coredata.dc:title: Construction of carbon dots-deposited TiO2 Photocatalysts with visible-light-induced photocatalytic activity for the elimination of pollutants
coredata.dc:creator: Shen, Shen
Chen, Kunlin
Wang, Hongbo
Fu, Jiajia
coredata.dc:description: Element decomposition is a promising modification technique for enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2. Firstly, (i) It may accelerate photogenerated electron–hole separation as electron traps, (ii) broaden the sunlight absorption region into the visible range and promote surface electron excitation by plasmon resonances excited under visible light and (iii) modify the surface performance of semiconductors. Herein, we describe the introduction of carbon dots (CDs) as solid-state electron mediator on TiO2 sheets (TNs) via a highly effective hydrothermal process. The as-prepared CDs/TNs nanocomposites was measured by X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis. The results proved that CDs were firmly scattered in the TNs interface. Furthermore, the resultant CDs/TNs nanocomposites exhibited remarkable photocatalytic activity for the degradation of different model pollutants under visible light, which is considerably superior to that of bare TNs. The enhanced photocatalytic activity highlights that CDs can promote the separation and migration efficiency of photoexcited electron–hole pairs. The striking stability of CDs/TNs was investigated by performing five successive cycles of degradation of Congo red. Moreover, the degradation intermediates of three pollutants were monitored by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry, and potential removal pathways are proposed. We firmly believe that this work may inspire new insights and approaches for the design of highly efficient photocatalysts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Aquatic pollution
Photocatalysis
Degradation
Removal mechanism

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pathway and mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) TEM image of TNs; (b) enlarged TEM image of (a); TEM images of CDs (c) and 0.2CDs/TNs (d).
XRD patterns of CDs, TNs, and CDs/TNs with different CDs content.
FTIR spectra of CDs, TNs, and 0.2CDs/TNs.
XPS spectra of 0.2CDs/TNs: (a) survey XPS spectrum, (b) C1s, (c) O1s, (d) Ti2p.
UV–vis DRS spectra of TNs and CDs/TNs with different CDs content.
PL spectra of TNs and CDs/TNs with different CDs content.
EIS curves (a) and transient photocurrent density (b) of TNs and 0.2CDs/TNs under visible light irradiation.
Photodegradation of (a) Congo red, (b) RhB, (c) TC by CDs/TNs composites with different contents of CDs under visible light; (d) stability of 0.2CDs/TNs for Congo red removal.

Changes of the characteristic absorption after degradation by 0.2CDs/TNs under visible light irradiation of (a) CR and photographs of residual CR solution (inset); (b) RhB and photographs of residual RhB solution (inset); (c) TC and photographs of residual TC solution (inset).

(a) Possible pathways for the photocatalytic degradation of CR over the 0.2CDs/TNs photocatalyst under visible light irradiation. (b) Possible pathways for the photocatalytic degradation of RhB over the 0.2CDs/TNs photocatalyst under visible light irradiation. (c) Possible pathways for the photocatalytic degradation of TC over the 0.2CDs/TNs photocatalyst under visible light irradiation.
Schematic illustration of the synthesis procedure for Carbon dots/TiO2 sheets.
The schematic illustration of photocatalytic degradation mechanism of CDs-TNs.

head.ce:title: Construction of carbon dots-deposited TiO2 Photocatalysts with visible-light-induced photocatalytic activity for the elimination of pollutants

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Jiangsu Engineering Technology Research Centre for Functional Textiles, Jiangnan University, No. 1800 Lihu Avenue, Wuxi, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Engineering Technology Research Centre for Functional Textiles

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Eco-textiles, Ministry of Education, Jiangnan University, No. 1800 Lihu Avenue, Wuxi, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Eco-textiles, Ministry of Education, Jiangnan University, No.1800 Lihu Avenue, Wuxi, P.R. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Jiangsu Engineering Technology Research Centre for Functional Textiles, Jiangnan University, No. 1800 Lihu Avenue, Wuxi, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Jiangsu Engineering Technology Research Centre for Functional Textiles

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Element decomposition is a promising modification technique for enhancing the photocatalytic activity of TiO2. Firstly, (i) It may accelerate photogenerated electron–hole separation as electron traps, (ii) broaden the sunlight absorption region into the visible range and promote surface electron excitation by plasmon resonances excited under visible light and (iii) modify the surface performance of semiconductors. Herein, we describe the introduction of carbon dots (CDs) as solid-state electron mediator on TiO2 sheets (TNs) via a highly effective hydrothermal process. The as-prepared CDs/TNs nanocomposites was measured by X-ray diffraction, Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy, UV–visible diffuse reflectance spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy analysis. The results proved that CDs were firmly scattered in the TNs interface. Furthermore, the resultant CDs/TNs nanocomposites exhibited remarkable photocatalytic activity for the degradation of different model pollutants under visible light, which is considerably superior to that of bare TNs. The enhanced photocatalytic activity highlights that CDs can promote the separation and migration efficiency of photoexcited electron–hole pairs. The striking stability of CDs/TNs was investigated by performing five successive cycles of degradation of Congo red. Moreover, the degradation intermediates of three pollutants were monitored by liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry, and potential removal pathways are proposed. We firmly believe that this work may inspire new insights and approaches for the design of highly efficient photocatalysts.

ce:section.ce:para: Aquatic contamination poses a severe threat to water quality and is one of the main sources of chemical toxicity to humans [1]. In particular, textile dyes, easily recognized by their obvious color, are the major pollutants in aquatic environments, triggering disastrous impact on ecosystems integrity and civilization [2,3]. Wastewaters generated by industries and agriculture contain abundant nonfixed dyes and a huge amount of antibiotics contaminants. Therefore, the removal or degradation of pollutants has become a research hotspot for water purification. Among various types of conventional strategies including physical adsorption, coagulation, and extraction, photocatalysis stands out as a compelling technique for environment remediation, which converts weak solar energy into strong chemical energy [4–7].

In this context, TiO2 is an appealing photocatalyst for large-scale degradation of pollutants, not only due to its relatively low cost and high economic benefit but also because of its superior activity and chemical inertness [8,9]. However, pure TiO2 still has some drawbacks: (1) a large band gap of 3.2 eV that hinders its response to visible light, which severely restricts the conversion efficiency of solar energy; and (2) an uncontrolled recombination of electron (e−)-hole (h+) pairs that results in low quantum yields, thereby reducing the photocatalytic activity of pure TiO2 [10,11] . Numerous studies have been devoted to overcome such defects, resulting in a number of strategies that improve the photocatalytic activity of TiO2, including ion introduction [12–14], semiconductor or materials sensitization [15], and metal and nonmetal deposition [16]. In particular, the combination of carbon materials such as carbon nanotubes, grapheme [17], fullerenes, and nanodiamonds with photocatalysts can extend visible light absorption and accelerate the electron–hole pair separation, and hence can promote the degradation efficiency [16,18] . An absorption enhancement of TiO2 in the visible light region could improve its photocatalytic activity [19].

Carbon dots (CDs), which are burgeoning carbon materials regarded as alternative nonnoble metal cocatalysts, have sparked considerable attention and applications in photocatalysis. CDs possess robust chemical stability, small size, surface functional groups, special photoluminescence, and fantastic charge transport and charge acceptor properties [20,21]. Due to their extraordinary properties, CDs have been employed as modifiers to increase the photocatalytic performance [18] of diverse semiconductors such as BiWO6 [22], C3N4 [23], ZnO [24], and Ag3PO4 [25]. Besides, Liu et al. loaded single atom-dispersed Ag and CDs on the surface of C3N4, which resulted in higher photocatalytic degradation of NPX [26]. Huang et al. prepared CdS/CDs composites exhibiting excellent photocatalytic performance for H2 evolution [27]. Considering the striking advantages of CDs, we were interested in investigating the enhancement of the photocatalytic activity of TiO2 under visible light by decorating with CDs. Additionally, the maximization of photocatalytic performance by tuning the CDs loading amount, which is a current fundamental challenge, is addressed [28,29].

In this work, CDs/TNs catalysts were fabricated by interdispersing CDs in TiO2 sheets via a simple hydrothermal method (Scheme 1 ), and were thoroughly characterized by a series of techniques. As expected, the CDs/TNs catalysts exhibited a terrific performance in the decomposition of pollutants under visible light. Additionally, plausible pathways for the removal of Congo red (CR), Rhodamine B (RhB), and Tetracycline (TC) were investigated through the detection of intermediates by liquid chromatography–tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS). Finally, the underlying photodegradation mechanism of CDs/TNs was systematically clarified.

Tetrabutyl titanate (TBOT), ammonium citrate (AC), CR, and TC were purchased from Aladdin Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Hydrofluoric acid (HF), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), and RhB were provided by Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (China). Deionized (DI) water obtained using an ULUPURE pure ultrapure water/water system was used in all experiments.

The TNs were prepared via a previously reported hydrothermal process [30]. Briefly, 3 mL HF was added into 25 mL TBOT under constant stirring. Then, the mixture was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave at 180 °C for 24 h. Afterwards, the resulting sediments were filtrated and washed with NaOH and DI water, and then dried at 60 °C for 12 h in an oven.

First, 1.2161 g AC (0.2 M) was immersed into 25 mL DI water with stirring to achieve a uniform dispersion. Afterwards, the obtained solution was placed in a Teflon-lined autoclave and kept in an oven at 160 °C for 4 h. After the reaction, the solution was purified by centrifugation, followed by dialysis (Mw = 3500) to eliminate the residues. Finally, the CDs solution was freeze-dried, and the final product was collected as a solid.

Initially, 1.216 g AC (0.2 M) was added into 25 mL DI water. Subsequently, 0.4 g TiO2 was added to the as-formed solution with sonication and stirring. Next, the resultant solution was loaded into a Teflon-lined autoclave and maintained at 160 °C for 4 h to complete the hydrothermal process. The formed precipitates were centrifuged, washed with DI water, and dried at 60 °C for 12 h. CDs/TNs composites having different amounts of CDs (corresponding to 0.608, 1.216, 1.824, and 2.432 g AC), respectively abbreviated as 0.1CDs/TNs, 0.2CDs/TNs, 0.3CDs/TNs, and 0.4CDs/TNs, were prepared.

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted on a Thermo Nicolet IS10 spectrometer with scanning from 4000 to 500 cm−1. UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (DRS) of the solid were recorded using a UV-3600 (Agilent, Cary300) instrument with BaSO4 as a reference. The identification of phases was performed by X-ray diffraction (XRD, Bruker AXS) using Cu Kα radiation. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was carried out on a Thermo ESCALAB 250XI device. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were observed using a JEM-2010, JEOL instrument. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of the samples were recorded on an FS5 spectrofluorometer (Edinburgh Instruments).

Photocurrent measurements were performed on a CHI-760E electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., Ltd., China) using a conventional three-electrode system with 0.5 M Na2SO4 as the electrolyte solution, Ag/AgCl as the working electrode, and Pt wire as the counter electrode. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed using a 0.5 M Na2SO4 solution by applying a sinusoidal wave with an amplitude of 5 mV from 100 kHz to 0.1 Hz. Photocatalytic degradation experiments were conducted using CR, RhB, and TC as target pollutants under visible light irradiation. The visible light source was a 300 W (XO-CHX-ID) Xe lamp with a cutoff filter (≥420 nm) and the light intensity in the reactant center was around 25.6 mW/cm2. Briefly, 40 mg catalyst was suspended in 40 mL aqueous solution containing CR (120 mg L−1), RhB (20 mg L−1), or TC (80 mg L−1). Before irradiation, the reaction solution was placed in darkness to reach adsorption equilibrium. At fixed intervals, the degraded liquid was taken out and filtered to extract the catalyst. The obtained CR, RhB, or TC supernatants were subjected to UV–vis spectrophotometer.

The degraded intermediates and final products of CR, RhB, and TC were detected via LC-MS (Waters, USA) using a BEH C18 column (1.7 μm, 2.1 ∗ 150 mm). Further details are provided in the Supporting information.

Fig. 1 presents TEM images of the samples. As can be seen in the HRTEM image depicted in Fig. 1a-b, pure TNs display a flake-shaped morphology with an average diameter of approximately 30–60 nm, with an interplanar spacing of 0.2105 and 0.356 nm corresponding to the (001) and (101) planes of anatase TiO2, respectively. Fig. 1c shows that the synthesized CDs are spherical and well dispersed. Meanwhile, the TEM image of 0.2CDs/TNs (Fig. 1d) exhibits CDs uniformly distributed on the TNs surface, implying that CDs and TNs are a suitable couple.

The XRD patterns of the samples are provided in Fig. 2 . The distinct diffraction peak at 30.5° for CDs is ascribable to (200) disordered graphitic-like species [32]. For the TNs and the CDs/TNs composites, the characteristic peaks from 25.28° to 75.03° can be indexed to crystal planes of anatase TiO2 (PDF No. 00-021-1272). Nevertheless, no characteristic peak for CDs is observed in the patterns of the CDs/TNs catalysts, owing to the low CDs doping content [31–33]. Moreover, with increasing the CDs loading amount, no obvious shift of the distinct peaks of the CDs/TNs catalysts is observed, which suggests that the deposition of CDs has little influence on the crystal structure of the TNs [34].

FTIR spectra were recorded to identify the components and functional groups of the samples (Fig. 3 ). The absorption peaks centered at 3443, 1636, and 1328 cm−1 in all spectra can be ascribed to the CC/CO stretching vibration and bending vibration of hydroxyl groups of surface absorbed water, respectively [34]. In the FTIR spectrum of the CDs, the vibrational bands at 3244, 2922, and 1406 cm−1 are attributable to NH, CH, and CNbonds on the surface of the CDs. Besides, as can be seen in the spectrum of 0.2CDs/TNs, a peak due to a new COC bond appears at 1288 cm−1, and the peak at 1636 cm−1 is stronger than in the spectrum of the TNs [35–37], indicating the presence of CDs in the composites.

XPS was used as a powerful tool for further clarifying the chemical elements present in 0.2CDs/TNs. According to the survey XPS spectrum shown in Fig. 4a, C, O, Ti, and N are the dominant elements on the 0.2CDs/TNs interface. In the C1s region (Fig. 4b), the peaks at 283.7, 284.9, 286.3, and 287.1 eV, which correspond to CTi, CC & CC, CO, and CC, respectively, confirm the presence of CDs on the TNs [38,39]. In the O1s spectrum (Fig. 4c), the characteristic peaks situated at 528.7, 529.4, and 531.4 eV can be assigned to TiO, CO, HO, respectively, and indicate the presence of a TiOC chemical bond between CDs and TNs [40]. Two separate peaks at 457.6 and 463 eV can be ascribed to Ti2p3/2 and Ti2p1/2, respectively (Fig. 4d).

An enhancement of absorption in visible light is an essential factor to obtain improved photocatalytic activity [41,42]. In this study, the optical absorption of the prepared samples was evaluated using DRS, and the result is displayed in Fig. 5 . The individual TNs exhibit intrinsic absorption in the UV region. In contrast, the absorption of the CDs/TNs nanocomposites gradually extend into the visible region upon increasing the CDs content. The results indicate that the introduction of CDs onto TNs can promote visible light absorption, with the concomitant generation of more e−-h+ pairs for an enhanced degradation performance.

To investigate the favorable role of the CDs on the recombination of photogenerated e− and h+ on the TNs, the PL spectra of the TNs and the CDs/TNs nanocomposites were recorded. As shown in Fig. 6 , all samples possess a broad emission peak at 460–475 nm. The PL intensity of the CDs/TNs composites decreases distinctly compared with pure TNs, revealing that the interaction between CDs and TNs provides a rapid electron transfer pathway that greatly accelerates the photocatalytic reactions. Among these samples, the 0.2CDs/TNs catalyst shows the weaker PL peak intensity, indicating that CDs hamper the recombination of e−-h+ pairs and promote the photodegradation property of the TNs.

Next, photoelectrochemical measurements were employed to evaluate the charge transfer inside the TNs and 0.2CDs/TNs under visible light stimulation. The resulting EIS Nyquist plots are depicted in Fig. 7a. The semicircle diameter of 0.2CD/TNs is narrower than that of pure TNs, demonstrating a lower charge transfer resistance, which facilitates the charge transfer at the interface of 0.2CDs/TNs [26,43,44]. Moreover, the transient photocurrent responses are illustrated in Fig. 7b. The photocurrent density of TNs under intermittent visible irradiation is relatively lower, and therefore, it can be neglected. Interestingly, 0.2CDs/TNs presents a dramatically enhanced photocurrent response, revealing that the fabricated CDs can accelerate the migration rate of e−-h+ pairs, thereby contributing to the remarkable photodegradation performance [45]. Furthermore, 0.2CDs/TNs still maintains excellent photoelectrochemical stability even after five on/off cycles [46]. On the basis of these photoelectrochemical results, it can be concluded that the CDs were successfully loaded on the surface of TNs.

The photocatalytic performance of the samples was evaluated by degrading different pollutants, namely, CR (100 mg L−1), RhB (20 mg L−1), and TC (80 mg L−1), under visible light. Fig. 8a displays the photocatalytic activity of TNs and CDs/TNs for CR degradation. It can be clearly seen that pure TiO2 has no CR photodegradation activity when illuminated by visible light. In contrast, the CD/TNs nanocomposites show much higher photocatalytic activity and can degrade CR under visible light irradiation. Among all the CDs/TNs nanocomposites with various CDs content, 0.2CDs/TNs exhibits the best photodegradation activity, with a CR degradation rate up to 85.9% after 120 min irradiation. Concretely, as the CDs content increases from 0.1 to 0.2 M, the degradation rate increases from 45.97% to 85.9%, demonstrating that the CDs promote the photocatalytic activity of the TNs. However, upon further increasing the amounts of CDs over 0.2 M, the degradation activity evidently decreases. This abnormal phenomenon is likely to be caused by aggregation of the CDs on the TNs, which would reduce the number of active sites, thereby suppressing CR degradation [44,47,48].

Apart from CR, RhB and TC were also explored under visible light as representative pollutants. Despite the different photocatalytic capacity observed for the three pollutants, the photodegradation ability for each pollutant follows the sequence of 0.2CDs/TNs > 0.1 CDs/TNs > 0.3 CDs/TNs > 0.4 CDs/TNs (Fig. 8b, c). In addition, the stability and reusability of 0.2CDs/TNs were examined (Fig. 8d). After five consecutive recycling experiments for CR degradation, the photodegradation activity of 0.2CDs/TNs exhibits no obvious deactivation, confirming that 0.2CDs/TNs have high photostability and can be repeatedly used in practical applications.

Since the 0.2CDs/TNs nanocomposite exhibited the most notable photocatalytic degradation activity after introduction of the CDs onto the surface of TNs, 0.2CDs/TNs was selected for further experiments.

To evaluate the photocatalytic activity of CDs/TNs, the photodegradation of CR (100 mg L−1), RhB (20 mg L−1), and TC (80 mg L−1) by 0.2CDs/TNs was used as a model. The absorbance of a CR solution degraded by 0.2CDs/TNs at different reaction times is displayed in Fig. 9a. Two characteristic absorption peaks are observed at 498 and 375 nm. The absorption intensity at 498 nm decreases gradually as the reaction time increases, almost disappearing after 120 min, which reveals that CR is gradually degraded [49]. Meanwhile, the absorption peak at 375 nm decreases and redshifts, indicating that some of the CR is mineralized to small molecules [50]. The inset picture in Fig. 9a shows that the CR solution is discolored completely by degradation. The spectra resulting from the photocatalysis of the RhB dye are shown in Fig. 9b. As can be seen, the absorption intensities quickly decrease, illustrating that RhB is decomposed as the reaction proceeds [51]. Fig. 9c shows the decrease of the peak of TC under visible light illumination [52]. Taken together, these results suggest that CDs/TNs have excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of various organic pollutants.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pathway and mechanism of the photocatalytic reaction
ce:section.ce:para: The unstable compounds of CR, RhB, and TC at different reaction times were identified by LC-MS. Feasible structures and relative information of intermediates regarding CR are illustrated in Table S1. After degradation, a series of products were observed on the basis of the LC-MS. Products with m/z = 239.01 and 184.24 (4,4′-diaminobiphenyl) proved to be the cleavage of NN moieties. Intermediates m/z = 208.23 (naphthalenesulfonic acid) and 144.17 (naphthol) result from the oxidation of naphthalene. Products with m/z 158.21 (naphthoquinone) and 108.09 (benzoquinone) are proposed as further ring opening and mineralization of m/z 144.17 and 110.11. The peak at m/z 169.23 (4-aminobiphenyl) can be assigned to the cleavage of CC in a product with m/z 184.24, and the product m/z 94.11 (phenol) is the oxidation product of m/z 122.12 (benzoic acid).

Furthermore, the degradation pathways for RhB and TC were proposed (Fig. 10b, Fig. 10c), starting from the protonated RhB and TC molecular ions with m/z 443.23 and 444.15, respectively. The intermediates produced during the degradation process are listed in Table S2 and Table S3. All the above-mentioned fragments can be eventually degraded to CO2, H2O, and small molecules [53–55].

The photocatalytic mechanism of the CDs/TNs nanocomposites is briefly depicted in Scheme 2 . The band gap of CDs/TNs is displayed in Fig. S1. The e−-h+ pairs can be excited effectively under visible light irradiation. After electrons migrate quickly to the conductive band, holes remain on the valence band of TiO2. Then, an appropriate amount of photogenerated electrons transport to the CDs surface. Consequently, the electrons on the CDs consume the superficial oxygen to produce superoxide (•O2 −), which is involved in the degradation reaction. Moreover, the holes on the valence band of TiO2 combine with H2O or OH− to form •OH, which would directly degrade the pollutants [52].

In summary, we have designed CDs/TNs composites comprised of CDs decorated on TNs via a hydrothermal route. In comparison with pure TNs, all the CDs/TNs nanocomposites present superior activity and photostability toward pollutants degradation under visible light irradiation. The improved catalytic performance is originated from the combination of the highly reactive (001) facets of TNs and the benefits of CDs. On the basis of LC-MS experiments, the intermediates and pathways for the degradation of RhB, CR, and TC were elucidated. It was concluded that CDs serve as electron acceptor to capture redundant electrons from the conduction band of the TNs, thereby impeding photoinduced electron-hole pair recombination and enhancing the photocatalytic performance of the CDs/Ns catalysts. We believe that our work may pave the way to develop novel strategies for the design of photocatalysts for environment remediation.

Organic dyes created during the industries untreated emission is a major issue not only owning to the hazardous effect on aquatic quality but also due to their toxicity and carcinogenicity. Photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants in natural sunlight and in actual water is of great significance.

Accordingly, we postulate that the use of GO as precursor could generate 3dGA with much enhanced photocatalytic activity for CDs-TNs. Compared to CDs-TNs, 3DGA@CDs-TNs exhibit remarkable photocatalytic degradation performance for Congo-red, Rhodamine B, and Tetracyclineunder visible-light. Furthermore, the wastewater was achieved by dyeing process. Afterwards, LC-MS/MS analysis of the reaction intermediates and products allowed us to propose plausible mechanisms and photodegradation pathways for the three model pollutants used.
Shen Shen: Conceptualization, Investigation, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Hongbo Wang: Writing – review & editing. Jiajia Fu: Writing – review & editing, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We gratefully acknowledge the financial support of Project funded by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31470509); China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (No. 2019T120390); Jiangsu Planned Projects for Postdoctoral Research funds (No. 2018K018A); the Opening Project of China National Textile and Apparel Council Key Laboratory of Natural Dyes, Soochow University (No. SDHY2122). The authors would also like to thank Shiyanjia lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for their professional tests support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.diamond.2022.108896.
